title: Mahinda's Bodhi Sapling Voyage
subtitle: How a Bodhi Branch Crossed the Sea and Rooted Two Kingdoms in One Dharma
era: Buddhist missions after the Third Council, c. 250-247 BCE
region: Pataliputra, Mihintale and Anuradhapura in Sri Lanka
moral: A peaceful mission can change the memory of nations.

sources.citation: Dipavamsa and Mahavamsa, Sri Lankan Pali chronicles
sources.note: Main narrative sources for the Third Council, Mahinda, Sanghamitra and the Bodhi sapling.

sources.citation: Rajagala inscription, Sri Lanka, c. 200 BCE
sources.note: Early Brahmi epigraphic corroboration of Mahinda's presence in Sri Lanka.

sources.citation: Nayanjot Lahiri, Ashoka in Ancient India (Harvard University Press, 2015); Wilhelm Geiger trans., Mahavamsa (1908)
sources.note: Modern and classic handling of the missions tradition.

panels.shot: sweeping wide shot from above, sea between India and Sri Lanka, Mahinda and Sanghamitra on opposite shores

dialogue.text: In Ashoka’s age of stone and resolve, Mahinda and Sanghamitta carried the Buddha’s path and a Bodhi branch across the sea, binding Lanka and the Ganga plains in quiet dharma.

panels.shot: wide establishing shot of ancient Pataliputra from river level

dialogue.text: Around 250 BCE, Pataliputra hummed with power. Ashoka’s empire stretched from the Hindu Kush to the Bay of Bengal, and the Ganga carried merchants, monks, and rumors of new reforms.

panels.shot: interior medium shot in a royal hall, emphasis on Ashokan pillars and sober gathering

dialogue.text: Sri Lankan chronicles later spoke of a Third Council at Pataliputra, guided by the elder Moggaliputta Tissa, where doctrine was debated and missions to distant lands took shape.

panels.shot: medium close-up on Mahinda & Sanghamitta side by side, contemplative

dialogue.text: The texts call Mahinda a son of Ashoka and Sanghamitta his daughter, though later scholars debate whether those ties were of blood or dharma. What endures is their role as bearers of the teaching.

panels.shot: low-angle shot of Ashokan pillar with lion capital, Mahinda & Sanghamitta in foreground

dialogue.speaker: Mahinda & Sanghamitta
dialogue.text: If the dharma is to live, it must walk beyond your frontiers.

panels.shot: wide shot of a map-like floor painting, feet stepping toward Lanka

dialogue.text: From this milieu, missions toward the northwest, the Himalaya, and the island of Lanka are remembered. In Sri Lanka’s chronicles, the voyage of Mahinda becomes the founding story of their Buddhism.

panels.shot: medium shot on Mahinda in a forest, Sanghamitta in the background, departure mood

dialogue.speaker: Sanghamitta
dialogue.text: Brother, the path we plant must be as enduring as this shade.

panels.shot: wide shot at river ghat, boats ready, monks embarking

dialogue.text: The tradition speaks of them traveling by stages southward. Mahinda’s path would lead to the rocky hill of Mihintale, Sanghamitta’s to a later voyage bearing a living branch of the Bodhi tree.

panels.shot: dramatic sea-level shot of boats on choppy water, sky vast

dialogue.text: Between the Coromandel coast and the northern shores of Lanka, their journeys crossed salt and wind. Later, these crossings would be remembered not as trade, but as dharma voyages.

panels.shot: wide establishing shot of Mihintale’s rocky hill and forest below

dialogue.text: On the island, the chronicles place Mahinda at Mihintale, the ‘Mahinda’s hill’, where rugged rock meets forest. Here, they say, he met the king Devanampiya Tissa while the ruler hunted deer.

panels.shot: medium shot of Mahinda facing Devanampiya Tissa, Sanghamitta slightly behind

dialogue.speaker: Mahinda & Sanghamitta
dialogue.text: Great king, can this hill, rich with life, be hunted forever without end?

panels.shot: close-up on Devanampiya Tissa lowering his bow, Mahinda & Sanghamitta serene

dialogue.text: The Mahavamsa tells of questions and answers, of the king’s mind turning from sport to ethics. Whether every exchange is exact or not, the memory is clear: a ruler paused, and listened.

panels.shot: medium group shot of monks teaching under a tree at Mihintale

dialogue.speaker: Mahinda & Sanghamitta
dialogue.text: Dharma is not conquest. It is restraint, compassion, and a mind made clear.

panels.shot: quiet interior shot of a simple shrine taking form, lamps lit

dialogue.text: Soon, according to tradition, monasteries rose. Mahinda became the teacher at the island’s first great sangha, and the royal house of Anuradhapura wove Buddhist rituals into the fabric of its rule.

panels.shot: extreme close-up of the Rajagala inscription on stone

dialogue.text: Centuries later, the Rajagala inscription, carved in early Brahmi, would quietly affirm that a monk named Mahinda was honored in Lanka, giving modern historians a hard trace beneath the stories.

panels.shot: medium shot on Sanghamitta preparing a Bodhi sapling in a courtyard

dialogue.text: The chronicles then tell of a second, gentler voyage. Sanghamitta, now a central figure in this link, is said to have carried a cutting from the Bodh Gaya tree itself to the king of Lanka.

panels.shot: wide shot of ship deck with Bodhi sapling, sea beyond

dialogue.speaker: Sanghamitta
dialogue.text: A single branch can carry a memory across generations and oceans.

panels.shot: ceremonial wide shot in Anuradhapura, crowds and royalty around the sapling

dialogue.text: At Anuradhapura, the sapling was planted with ceremony as the Sri Maha Bodhi. Chroniclers would later guard its story, recording storms, invasions, and kings, always noting that the tree still stood.

panels.shot: low-angle shot looking up through the Bodhi canopy, Mahinda & Sanghamitta silhouettes

dialogue.text: Today the Sri Maha Bodhi is honored as one of the oldest documented trees, its continuity recorded rather than cut for science. It stands as ritual memory of a moment when dharma crossed the sea.

panels.shot: wide contemporary-echo shot with modern pilgrims around the ancient tree

dialogue.text: However shaped by later monks, Mahinda and Sanghamitta’s remembered mission transformed Lanka’s history. A peaceful journey of words and a sapling became a bridge of faith between neighboring lands.
